knowledge.statement: A README file is the front door of a project. It should explain what the project does, why it exists, how to install it, how to use it, and how to contribute. A missing or poor README increases onboarding cost for every new developer.

Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) document the context, decision, and consequences of significant architectural choices. They create an institutional memory of why the system is built the way it is, preventing repeated debates about already-resolved questions.

An ADR should have a status (proposed, accepted, deprecated, superseded), context (the situation that necessitated the decision), decision (what was chosen), and consequences (trade-offs and effects of the decision).
API documentation should be generated from the source of truth — the API definition or code annotations — not maintained manually. Manually maintained docs drift out of sync with the actual API.

OpenAPI (formerly Swagger) is the standard specification for describing RESTful APIs. A valid OpenAPI document enables automatic generation of documentation, client SDKs, server stubs, and validation middleware.

Code comments should explain why, not what. The code already shows what it does; comments that just restate the code add noise. Comments should explain intent, constraints, trade-offs, and non-obvious logic.

A CHANGELOG is a file that records notable changes in each release in reverse chronological order. It should be written for users, not for developers — focus on what changed and why it matters, not what commits were made.
Keep a Changelog (keepachangelog.com) recommends grouping changes by type: Added, Changed, Deprecated, Removed, Fixed, Security. Each release section includes the version number and release date.

Conventional Commits is a specification for commit message format: type(scope): description. Types include feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, and perf. It enables automated changelog generation and semantic versioning.

Semantic Versioning (SemVer) defines version numbers as MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH. MAJOR increments on breaking changes, MINOR on new backwards-compatible features, and PATCH on backwards-compatible bug fixes.

Docstrings document functions, classes, and modules within the source code. They should describe the purpose, parameters, return values, and raised exceptions. They serve as the source of generated API reference documentation.

Documentation-as-Code treats documentation like source code: stored in version control, reviewed in pull requests, tested for correctness, and published via CI/CD. It keeps docs close to the code and in sync with changes.

The Diátaxis framework organises documentation into four types: tutorials (learning-oriented), how-to guides (task-oriented), reference (information-oriented), and explanation (understanding-oriented). Mixing types confuses readers.

A Getting Started guide should take a new user from zero to a working example in the shortest possible time. Every required step must be included; assumed knowledge is the most common cause of abandonment.

A runbook documents the operational procedures for a system: how to deploy, how to roll back, how to respond to specific alerts, and how to perform maintenance tasks. It reduces reliance on tribal knowledge during incidents.
A playbook extends the runbook concept with decision trees and escalation paths for incident response. It guides on-call engineers through diagnosing and resolving specific types of failure.
Self-documenting code uses descriptive names for variables, functions, and classes so that the code reads like prose. When code clearly expresses intent, comments become less necessary.

Documentation rot occurs when documentation becomes outdated as the system changes. It is best prevented by co-locating docs with code, reviewing docs in the same PR as code changes, and removing docs for deleted features.
Inline examples in documentation dramatically increase comprehension. A minimal working code example is worth many paragraphs of prose explanation.

Error messages are a form of documentation. They should tell the user what went wrong, why it happened, and what to do to fix it. Cryptic error messages like 'Error 42' push users to forums and slow down debugging.

A system design document (SDD) describes the high-level design of a system or feature before implementation. It enables early feedback on architecture decisions and serves as a reference throughout development.

A Request for Comments (RFC) process gathers feedback on a proposed change before implementation. It prevents rework by surfacing concerns and alternatives early, and creates a record of why a design was chosen.

Type annotations and typed interfaces serve as a form of machine-checked documentation. They communicate the expected types of function parameters and return values and are automatically enforced by type checkers and IDEs.

Literate programming interweaves code and documentation in a single document, where prose explains the code in the order that humans understand rather than the order the compiler requires. It is rarely used in production but influenced many documentation tools.

A Postmortem (or Incident Review) documents what happened during an incident, the timeline, root cause, impact, and corrective actions. Blameless postmortems focus on systemic improvements rather than individual fault.
README badges provide at-a-glance status information (build status, test coverage, licence, version). They give potential contributors immediate context about project health.

CONTRIBUTING.md documents how to set up a development environment, the branching strategy, code style requirements, how to run tests, and the pull request process. It reduces friction for first-time contributors.
A SECURITY.md file documents the security policy for a project: which versions receive security fixes, how to report vulnerabilities (responsible disclosure process), and expected response timelines.

Mermaid and PlantUML enable diagrams to be defined as text in source control alongside code. Diagrams defined as code do not suffer from documentation rot because they are versioned and can be diffed.

C4 model (Context, Containers, Components, Code) provides a hierarchical set of software architecture diagrams at increasing levels of detail. Starting with the context diagram ensures the system's place in the broader landscape is clear.

A Glossary of domain terms eliminates ambiguity in communication between developers and domain experts. When the same word means different things to different people, it causes bugs that are difficult to attribute to misunderstanding.
Documentation should be versioned alongside the code. When users of an older version encounter a problem, the documentation for that version should be available, not just the latest.

Dead documentation — documentation for features that no longer exist or behave differently — is worse than no documentation because it actively misleads users. Deleting outdated documentation requires discipline.

The four types of documentation audiences are: new developers (onboarding), experienced developers (reference), operators (runbooks), and end users (user guides). Each audience needs different content and detail level.
Link rot degrades documentation quality over time as external URLs become invalid. Prefer stable references (GitHub permalinks, archived versions) and periodically check links with automated tools.

Embedding documentation in the project repository rather than a separate wiki ensures that docs changes are reviewed in the same pull request as code changes and are deployed together with the code they describe.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) reduce support burden by pre-answering common questions. They should be based on actual questions, not hypothetical ones, and updated as new questions recur.

Migration guides document breaking changes between major versions and provide step-by-step instructions for upgrading. They are critical for keeping users on current versions and reducing support requests.
Swagger UI and Redoc render OpenAPI specifications as interactive documentation that allows developers to explore and test API endpoints directly in the browser without a separate client tool.

Test code is documentation. Well-written tests show how to use a class or function, what the expected behaviour is in various conditions, and what edge cases exist. Tests that are hard to read fail as documentation.

Documentation debt accumulates when teams skip writing docs under time pressure. Like technical debt, documentation debt compounds over time: undocumented decisions lead to undocumented code, which leads to undocumented systems.
Tutorials should be opinionated and prescriptive: tell the user exactly what to do, not all the ways they could do it. Providing choices in tutorials overwhelms beginners and slows learning.
Reference documentation should be comprehensive and structured for lookup, not narrative reading. It answers 'what does X do?' and should cover every parameter, return value, and error condition.

How-to guides address specific tasks ('How do I add pagination?') and assume the reader has basic familiarity with the system. They differ from tutorials (which build understanding) and reference docs (which describe everything).

Explanation documents provide background context, discuss trade-offs, and explain the 'why' behind design decisions. They help readers build a mental model of the system rather than just know how to use it.
Public APIs should never change behaviour without updating documentation first. API documentation that lags behind implementation breaks the trust of API consumers and makes integrations fragile.

Database schema documentation should describe the purpose of each table and column, not just the data type. A column named 'status' with values 0, 1, 2 is undecipherable without documentation of what each value means.

Environment variable documentation should list every required variable, its purpose, valid values, default values, and whether it is optional or required. Missing this documentation is a common cause of deployment failures.
